story:
Screams coil through the frozen halls beneath Admor Keep, the bright face of the “great” nation of Hace. Shackled, bruised, and bleeding, Caelin—once a knife in the dark—is dragged by two Admor guards, his head knocking off stone. An arrow in his calf ended the long hunt. They call him a serial killer of innocents. He insists his targets were corrupt political figures, but the blood on his hands is real.

Stripped of his tools and thrown into coarse prison cotton, he stumbles past cells where desperate hands reach for him. A filthy, long-haired prisoner lunges and clamps an arm around his neck, whispering, “Ohh they’re going to love having you.” The taller guard tears Caelin free, storms into the cell, and, with cold fury, stabs the man in the throat. The body collapses, eyes empty. Caelin doesn’t flinch. Death has long been a language he understands—even if that makes him monstrous.

“Touch him again and I’ll rip you apart,” the taller guard roars down the corridor, then, quieter, promises the prisoners their turn when “we’re done with him.” Between the guards’ cruelty and Caelin’s crimes, the dungeon reeks of rot from top to bottom. For the first time in years, fear grips Caelin. He has prepared death for others, never for himself. Now the corridors fall silent, the hands withdraw, and the echo of his own footsteps makes him feel human again—and terribly alone.

They round a final corner. No cells. No turns. Only a room. The smaller guard leans into Caelin’s face, hissing, “I can’t believe I’m looking into your filthy eyes. Now, you’ve got a little surprise.” He headbutts him, spits, and shoves him into the chamber. A blow drops Caelin to the stone. Boots find his ribs, and he is hauled upright by the throat to face an elderly man with long gray hair: the captain of the guard.

Bound to the chair, Caelin watches the captain pour wine, then set tools on a table—some familiar, others wicked and strange. “My daughter, son, nephew, and grandson are gone because of you,” the man says. “I will avenge them.” Caelin’s head swims. Whispering rises in his ears, faint and familiar, like voices from a past life.

A scream rips through the corridor outside. More follow, growing like a storm. The captain whirls for the door, which has somehow sealed shut. “Magic?” he spits. “Scum from across the borders.” He pounds the door, then returns to Caelin with a blade like a sword ringed by a razor loop. He lifts it, smiling, as black mist seeps under the threshold and fills the room like a tide.

Caelin’s shackles loosen by themselves and clatter to the floor. The captain steps back, stunned, then drives forward—only to be impaled from behind by a spear of shadow. He is lifted, choking, as the cloud engulfs the chamber. Caelin rises with open arms, greeted by the darkness like an old friend. It wraps him, hardens into an ethereal dagger in his fist. He kneels the captain down and slides the blade across his throat—slow, deliberate, almost gentle.

“No,” Caelin rasps, voice warped by the thing that holds him. “We are the end.”

The screams outside fade. The dungeon is silent again, the shining keep above as false as ever, while down below, something older than law and colder than mercy answers to Caelin—and he to it.
